result.markdown:
--- title: "Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis" subtitle: "Meta-Analytic Update of Mate-Choice Copying (MCC)" author: "Eduardo S. A. Santos & Collaborators" date: today format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 theme: cosmo self-contained: true --- ## Introduction & Project Update This protocol implements the overall effect size synthesis for the updated Mate-Choice Copying (MCC) meta-analysis. Specifically, we integrate: 1. **The New Extractions:** Our newly extracted and verified dataset containing recent trials. 2. **Original Continuous Dataset (@daviesMetaanalysisFactorsInfluencing2020):** Standardised mean differences (Hedges' $d$) analyzing continuous metrics. 3. **Original Binary Dataset (@jonesMechanismsSocialInfluence2019):** Log Odds Ratio ($\ln\text{OR}$) analyzing binary choices. The R script aligns and synthesizes these three sources into unified datasets, allowing comparison of meta-analytic summaries across three key subsets: * **New Extractions Only:** Eduardo's new dataset. * **Original Only:** Legacy data from the original publications. * **Combined Dataset:** A complete synthesis of old and new trials to estimate the updated global effect sizes. --- ## Data Loading and Alignment We read the three datasets, perform data type conversions, extract species epithets for taxonomic control, and align columns into unified Hedges' $g$ and Odds Ratio datasets. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 1. Load Datasets # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Paths to legacy and new datasets path_new <- here::here("data", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline", "Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx") path_davies <- here::here("data", "2_original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Davies_et_al_2020_Final_data.xlsx") path_jones <- here::here("data", "2_original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Jones_DuVal_2019_data.CSV") # A. Read new extraction dataset (EduardoExtraction sheet) db_new_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_new, sheet = "EduardoExtraction") # B. Read Davies et al. 2020 (Original continuous dataset) db_davies_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_davies, sheet = "Data") # C. Read Jones & DuVal 2019 (Original binary dataset) db_jones_raw <- readr::read_csv(path_jones) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 2. Process and Filter New Extraction # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- db_new <- db_new_raw %>% filter(is.na(RemoveFromDataset) | RemoveFromDataset != "Yes") %>% mutate( effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance), effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio), effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance), logOddsRatio = log(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio), logOddsRatioVariance = effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance ) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 3. Align Datasets for Hedges' g (Continuous SMD Analysis) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # A. Hedges' g from New Extraction hedges_new <- db_new %>% filter(!is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), !is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance)) %>% select( yi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD, vi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), # Extract species epithet (last word) and make lowercase for cross-dataset mapping taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)" ) # B. Hedges' g from Davies et al. 2020 (Original) hedges_davies <- db_davies_raw %>% mutate( Hedges_d_directional = as.numeric(Hedges_d_directional), Variance = as.numeric(Variance) ) %>% filter(!is.na(Hedges_d_directional), !is.na(Variance), Variance > 0) %>% select( yi = Hedges_d_directional, vi = Variance, identifierStudyId = Author, identifierEffectSizeID = Effect_size_no, taxonomySpecies = Species_latin ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), # Extract species epithet (last word) and make lowercase taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "Original (Davies 2020)" ) # C. Unified Hedges' g Dataset db_hedges <- bind_rows(hedges_new, hedges_davies) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source), as.factor)) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 4. Align Datasets for Odds Ratio (Binary Analysis) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # A. Log Odds Ratio from New Extraction or_new <- db_new %>% filter(!is.na(logOddsRatio), !is.na(logOddsRatioVariance)) %>% select( yi = logOddsRatio, vi = logOddsRatioVariance, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)" ) # B. Log Odds Ratio from Jones & DuVal 2019 (Original) or_jones <- db_jones_raw %>% filter(!is.na(lnOR), !is.na(VlnOR), VlnOR > 0) %>% select( yi = lnOR, vi = VlnOR, identifierStudyId = study, identifierEffectSizeID = study_Exp, taxonomySpecies = Species ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), Dataset_Source = "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" ) # C. Unified Log Odds Ratio Dataset db_or <- bind_rows(or_new, or_jones) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source), as.factor)) ``` ::: --- ## Dataset Composition Summary {#sec-composition} Before inspecting the effect size distributions, we summarize how the aligned datasets are composed. The first table reports the number of studies (`k`) and effect sizes contributed by each original source and by our new extraction. The second table summarizes the number of effect sizes per species, ordered from most to least common. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Combine both aligned effect-size datasets, tagging the effect-size class. # Note: the New Extraction (Santos) contributes to BOTH the Hedges' g and the # Odds Ratio datasets; n_distinct() on study IDs therefore avoids double-counting # studies while total effect sizes are summed across classes. db_summary_all <- bind_rows( db_hedges %>% mutate(EffectType = "Hedges' g"), db_or %>% mutate(EffectType = "Log Odds Ratio") ) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source, EffectType), as.character)) ``` ::: ### Studies & Effect Sizes by Source ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Effect Sizes per Species ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: In total, the aligned data comprise 399 effect sizes from 120 studies across 36 species. The most-studied species is *Melanogaster* (113 effect sizes from 22 studies). --- ## Effect Size Distribution (Density Plots) {#sec-density} Before fitting the multi-level models, we inspect the distribution of our aligned effect sizes across the different subsets (New Extraction, Original Only, and Combined). The density plots below use the same split logic as the orchard plots in the result section, facet-wrapping by subset while coloring the distribution curves by their actual dataset source to check for shifts or outliers. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Hedges' $g$ (Continuous SMD) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Prepare density data for Hedges' g db_hedges_density <- bind_rows( db_hedges %>% mutate(Group = as.character(Dataset_Source)), db_hedges %>% mutate(Group = "Combined") ) %>% mutate(Group = factor(Group, levels = c("New Extraction (Santos)", "Original (Davies 2020)", "Combined"))) # Plot densities p_hedges_dens <- ggplot(db_hedges_density, aes(x = yi, fill = Dataset_Source, color = Dataset_Source)) + geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) + geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "solid", color = "black") + geom_rug(alpha = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~Group, ncol = 1) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + scale_color_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + labs( title = "Distribution of Hedges' g Effect Sizes", x = "Hedges' g", y = "Density" ) + theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + theme( legend.position = "bottom", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5) ) print(p_hedges_dens) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/hedges_density_plots-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ### Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Prepare density data for Odds Ratio (lnOR) db_or_density <- bind_rows( db_or %>% mutate(Group = as.character(Dataset_Source)), db_or %>% mutate(Group = "Combined") ) %>% mutate(Group = factor(Group, levels = c("New Extraction (Santos)", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)", "Combined"))) # Plot densities p_or_dens <- ggplot(db_or_density, aes(x = yi, fill = Dataset_Source, color = Dataset_Source)) + geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) + geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "solid", color = "black") + geom_rug(alpha = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~Group, ncol = 1) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + scale_color_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + labs( title = "Distribution of Log Odds Ratio (lnOR) Effect Sizes", x = "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", y = "Density" ) + theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + theme( legend.position = "bottom", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5) ) print(p_or_dens) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/or_density_plots-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: --- ## Model Fitting Function This helper function handles the variance–covariance (VCV) matrix construction and fits a multilevel random-effects model using `rma.mv()` in `metafor`. * **Variance–Covariance:** We construct a VCV matrix via `vcalc()` to handle dependencies among multiple effect sizes within the same study (assuming $r = 0.5$ for nested measurements). * **Random intercepts** are modeled for: * Study (`identifierStudyId`) * Individual trial/effect size (`identifierEffectSizeID`) * Species (`taxonomySpecies`) to control for taxonomic/species-level variation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fit_overall_mv <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) { # Remove rows with missing values dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) { message("No complete cases found.") return(NULL) } # 1. Build VCV Matrix VCV <- metafor::vcalc( vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2 ) # 2. Fit Multi-Level Random-Effects Model mod <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, random = list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = mod) } # Helper function to fit bias-corrected multilevel models with a continuous moderator (Egger-type) fit_corrected_mv <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", moderator = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) { # Remove rows with missing values dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) { message("No complete cases found.") return(NULL) } if (moderator == "se" && !"se" %in% colnames(dat2)) { dat2 <- dat2 %>% mutate(se = sqrt(vi)) } # 1. Build VCV Matrix VCV <- metafor::vcalc( vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2 ) # 2. Fit Multi-Level Egger Model using dynamic formula formula_str <- paste("~ 1 +", moderator) mod <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, mods = as.formula(formula_str), random = list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = mod) } # Helper function to add Mean (95% CI) labels dynamically one line above k-values in orchard plots add_ci_labels <- function(plot_obj, orchard_results) { est <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$estimate) ci_l <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$lowerCL) ci_u <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$upperCL) orig_labels <- plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$label new_labels <- c() for (i in 1:length(orig_labels)) { # Extract the original sample size numbers using regex nums <- regmatches(orig_labels[i], gregexpr("[0-9]+", orig_labels[i]))[[1]] if (length(nums) >= 2) { k_val <- nums[1] n_val <- nums[2] k_text <- paste0("k = ", k_val, " (", n_val, ")") } else { k_text <- orig_labels[i] } # Place Mean (95% CI) on the first line and k = xx (xx) on the second line new_labels[i] <- paste0(est[i], " [", ci_l[i], ", ", ci_u[i], "]\n", k_text) } plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$label <- new_labels plot_obj$layers[[6]]$geom_params$parse <- FALSE plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$hjust <- "right" plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$vjust <- 0.5 plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$size <- 3.0 # Slightly smaller font size to fit better plot_obj } ``` ::: --- ## Multi-Level Synthesis Results We fit the multilevel meta-analysis across all three database splits (New Only, Original Only, and Combined) for both effect size classes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # HEDGES' G MODELS (UNCORRECTED) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- res_hedges_new <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)")) res_hedges_orig <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)")) res_hedges_comb <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges) # HEDGES' G MODELS (BIAS-CORRECTED) res_hedges_new_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), moderator = "vi") res_hedges_orig_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), moderator = "vi") res_hedges_comb_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges, moderator = "vi") # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # ODDS RATIO MODELS (UNCORRECTED) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- res_or_new <- fit_overall_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)")) res_or_orig <- fit_overall_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)")) res_or_comb <- fit_overall_mv(db_or) # ODDS RATIO MODELS (BIAS-CORRECTED) res_or_new_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), moderator = "vi") res_or_orig_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), moderator = "vi") res_or_comb_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or, moderator = "vi") ``` ::: --- ## Results and Visualizations ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Hedges' $g$ (Continuous outcomes) ::: {.panel-tabset} #### Uncorrected Overall Effect This plot displays the standard multi-level meta-analytic summaries across the three splits (New Only, Original Only, and Combined) *without* adjusting for publication bias. Points represent individual effect sizes colored by their data source. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_hedges_new) && !is.null(res_hedges_orig) && !is.null(res_hedges_comb)) { # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results h_new <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new$model, group = "identifierStudyId") h_orig <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId") h_comb <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_comb$model, group = "identifierStudyId") # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing class(h_new) <- "list" h_new$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)" h_new$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)" class(h_new) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(h_orig) <- "list" h_orig$mod_table$name <- "Original (Davies 2020)" h_orig$data$moderator <- "Original (Davies 2020)" class(h_orig) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(h_comb) <- "list" h_comb$mod_table$name <- "Combined" h_comb$data$moderator <- "Combined" class(h_comb) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 3. Merge model results sub_hedges <- orchaRd::submerge(h_new, h_orig, h_comb) # 4. Add point-level real source mapping class(sub_hedges) <- "list" sub_hedges$data$real_source <- c( rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new$data)), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig$data)), rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new$data)), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig$data)) ) class(sub_hedges) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 4. Generate Plot p_hedges <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(sub_hedges, xlab = "Hedges' g", flip = TRUE) # 5. Customize color mapping for points p_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill # 6. Apply themes & custom palette (colorblind friendly orange/blue) p_hedges <- p_hedges + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle("Overall Synthesis — Hedges' g Estimates (Uncorrected)") + ggplot2::theme( legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02), legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"), legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"), legend.box = "vertical", legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4), legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"), legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8) ) # Add Mean (95% CI) and k labels p_hedges <- add_ci_labels(p_hedges, sub_hedges) print(p_hedges) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/hedges_unified_plot-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: #### Bias-Corrected Overall Effect This plot displays the small-study publication bias corrected meta-analytic summaries (evaluated at $\text{V} = 0$, representing the theoretical estimate for a study of infinite sample size/precision) using the two-step Egger-type meta-regression framework. Points represent the same raw effect sizes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_hedges_new_corr) && !is.null(res_hedges_orig_corr) && !is.null(res_hedges_comb_corr)) { # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results at vi = 0 h_new_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") h_orig_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") h_comb_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_comb_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing class(h_new_corr) <- "list" h_new_corr$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)" h_new_corr$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)" class(h_new_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(h_orig_corr) <- "list" h_orig_corr$mod_table$name <- "Original (Davies 2020)" h_orig_corr$data$moderator <- "Original (Davies 2020)" class(h_orig_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(h_comb_corr) <- "list" h_comb_corr$mod_table$name <- "Combined" h_comb_corr$data$moderator <- "Combined" class(h_comb_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 3. Merge model results sub_hedges_corr <- orchaRd::submerge(h_new_corr, h_orig_corr, h_comb_corr) # 4. Add point-level real source mapping class(sub_hedges_corr) <- "list" sub_hedges_corr$data$real_source <- c( rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new_corr$data)), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig_corr$data)), rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new_corr$data)), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig_corr$data)) ) class(sub_hedges_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 4. Generate Plot p_hedges_corr <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(sub_hedges_corr, xlab = "Hedges' g (Bias Corrected)", flip = TRUE) # 5. Customize color mapping for points p_hedges_corr$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_hedges_corr$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill # 6. Apply themes & custom palette p_hedges_corr <- p_hedges_corr + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle("Overall Synthesis — Hedges' g Estimates (Bias-Corrected)") + ggplot2::theme( legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02), legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"), legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"), legend.box = "vertical", legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4), legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"), legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8) ) # Add Mean (95% CI) and k labels p_hedges_corr <- add_ci_labels(p_hedges_corr, sub_hedges_corr) print(p_hedges_corr) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/hedges_corrected_plot-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: #### Side-by-Side Comparison We combine the uncorrected and small-study bias corrected orchard plots side-by-side using `patchwork` to directly evaluate the shifts in pooled estimates and prediction intervals when publication selection is accounted for. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (exists("p_hedges") && exists("p_hedges_corr")) { comparison_hedges <- (p_hedges + p_hedges_corr) + patchwork::plot_annotation( title = "Comparison of Uncorrected vs. Bias-Corrected Synthesis (Hedges' g)", subtitle = "Bias-corrected estimates are conditioned on study variance = 0 (infinite precision)", theme = ggplot2::theme( plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_text(size = 12, italic = TRUE, hjust = 0.5) ) ) print(comparison_hedges) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/hedges_comparison_plot-1.png){width=1344} ::: ::: #### Detailed Model Statistics Here are the text outputs and fit statistics for the three database splits. Each split displays the uncorrected and small-study bias-corrected (Egger's variance-adjusted) model summaries: ::: {.panel-tabset} ##### New Only ::: {.panel-tabset} ###### Uncorrected ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_hedges_new)) { print(summary(res_hedges_new$model)) cat("\nHeterogeneity (I^2):\n") print(orchaRd::i2_ml(res_hedges_new$model)) } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 69; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -53.6358 107.2716 115.2716 124.1496 115.9065 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0000 0.0000 29 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.1509 0.3885 69 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.0865 0.2941 15 no taxonomySpecies Test for Heterogeneity: Q(df = 68) = 313.8235, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub 0.2069 0.1124 1.8413 68 0.0699 -0.0173 0.4311 . --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 Heterogeneity (I^2): I2_Total I2_identifierStudyId I2_identifierEffectSizeID 8.075307e+01 1.153766e-07 5.133174e+01 I2_taxonomySpecies 2.942133e+01 ``` ::: ::: ###### Bias-Corrected ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_hedges_new_corr)) { print(summary(res_hedges_new_corr$model)) cat("\nHeterogeneity (I^2):\n") print(orchaRd::i2_ml(res_hedges_new_corr$model)) } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 69; Points represent individual log odds ratios colored by their data source. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_or_new) && !is.null(res_or_orig) && !is.null(res_or_comb)) { # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results o_new <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_new$model, group = "identifierStudyId") o_orig <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId") o_comb <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_comb$model, group = "identifierStudyId") # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing class(o_new) <- "list" o_new$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)" o_new$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)" class(o_new) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(o_orig) <- "list" o_orig$mod_table$name <- "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" o_orig$data$moderator <- "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" class(o_orig) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(o_comb) <- "list" o_comb$mod_table$name <- "Combined" o_comb$data$moderator <- "Combined" class(o_comb) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 3. Merge model results sub_or <- orchaRd::submerge(o_new, o_orig, o_comb) # 4. Add point-level real source mapping class(sub_or) <- "list" sub_or$data$real_source <- c( rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(o_new$data)), rep("Original (Jones & DuVal)", nrow(o_orig$data)), rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(o_new$data)), rep("Original (Jones & DuVal)", nrow(o_orig$data)) ) class(sub_or) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 4. Generate Plot p_or <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(sub_or, xlab = "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", flip = TRUE) # 5. Customize color mapping for points p_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill # 6. Apply themes & custom palette (colorblind friendly orange/blue) p_or <- p_or + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle("Overall Synthesis — Log Odds Ratio Estimates (Uncorrected)") + ggplot2::theme( legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02), legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"), legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"), legend.box = "vertical", legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4), legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"), legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8) ) # Add Mean (95% CI) and k labels p_or <- add_ci_labels(p_or, sub_or) print(p_or) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/or_unified_plot-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: #### Bias-Corrected Overall Effect This plot displays the small-study publication bias corrected meta-analytic summaries (evaluated at $\text{V} = 0$, representing the theoretical estimate for a study of infinite sample size/precision) using the two-step Egger-type meta-regression framework. Points represent the same raw effect sizes. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (!is.null(res_or_new_corr) && !is.null(res_or_orig_corr) && !is.null(res_or_comb_corr)) { # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results at vi = 0 o_new_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_new_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") o_orig_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_orig_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") o_comb_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_comb_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1") # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing class(o_new_corr) <- "list" o_new_corr$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)" o_new_corr$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)" class(o_new_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(o_orig_corr) <- "list" o_orig_corr$mod_table$name <- "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" o_orig_corr$data$moderator <- "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" class(o_orig_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") class(o_comb_corr) <- "list" o_comb_corr$mod_table$name <- "Combined" o_comb_corr$data$moderator <- "Combined" class(o_comb_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 3. Merge model results sub_or_corr <- orchaRd::submerge(o_new_corr, o_orig_corr, o_comb_corr) # 4. Add point-level real source mapping class(sub_or_corr) <- "list" sub_or_corr$data$real_source <- c( rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(o_new_corr$data)), rep("Original (Jones & DuVal)", nrow(o_orig_corr$data)), rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(o_new_corr$data)), rep("Original (Jones & DuVal)", nrow(o_orig_corr$data)) ) class(sub_or_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame") # 4. Generate Plot p_or_corr <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(sub_or_corr, xlab = "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR) (Bias Corrected)", flip = TRUE) # 5. Customize color mapping for points p_or_corr$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_or_corr$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill # 6. Apply themes & custom palette p_or_corr <- p_or_corr + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle("Overall Synthesis — Log Odds Ratio Estimates (Bias-Corrected)") + ggplot2::theme( legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02), legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"), legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"), legend.box = "vertical", legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4), legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"), legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8) ) # Add Mean (95% CI) and k labels p_or_corr <- add_ci_labels(p_or_corr, sub_or_corr) print(p_or_corr) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/or_corrected_plot-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: #### Side-by-Side Comparison We combine the uncorrected and small-study bias corrected orchard plots side-by-side using `patchwork` to directly evaluate the shifts in pooled estimates and prediction intervals when publication selection is accounted for. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (exists("p_or") && exists("p_or_corr")) { comparison_or <- (p_or + p_or_corr) + patchwork::plot_annotation( title = "Comparison of Uncorrected vs. Bias-Corrected Synthesis (Odds Ratio)", subtitle = "Bias-corrected estimates are conditioned on study variance = 0 (infinite precision)", theme = ggplot2::theme( plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_text(size = 12, italic = TRUE, hjust = 0.5) ) ) print(comparison_or) } ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](overall_effect_files/figure-html/or_comparison_plot-1.png){width=1344} ::: ::: #### Detailed Model Statistics Here are the text outputs and fit statistics for the three database splits. Ignoring within-study correlation can lead to improper weighting of studies with multiple effect sizes and can bias the standard errors and confidence intervals of the pooled estimates. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # 1. Define comparison function compare_vcv_models <- function(dat, name, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) { dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) { return(NULL) } # A. With VCV (rho = 0.5) VCV <- metafor::vcalc( vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2 ) mod_w_vcv <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, random = list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) # B. Without VCV (diagonal V = vi) mod_no_vcv <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = dat2[[yi]], V = dat2[[vi]], random = list( ~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies ), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2 ) # Combine results data.frame( Analysis = name, Model_Type = c("With VCV (rho = 0.5)", "Without VCV (Independent)"), k = c(mod_w_vcv$k, mod_no_vcv$k), Estimate = c(mod_w_vcv$b[1], mod_no_vcv$b[1]), SE = c(mod_w_vcv$se[1], mod_no_vcv$se[1]), CI_Lower = c(mod_w_vcv$ci.lb[1], mod_no_vcv$ci.lb[1]), CI_Upper = c(mod_w_vcv$ci.ub[1], mod_no_vcv$ci.ub[1]), p_val = c(mod_w_vcv$pval[1], mod_no_vcv$pval[1]) ) } # 2. Run comparisons across all subsets comp_hedges_new <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), "Hedges' g (New Only)") comp_hedges_orig <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), "Hedges' g (Original Only)") comp_hedges_comb <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges, "Hedges' g (Combined)") comp_or_new <- compare_vcv_models(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), "Log OR (New Only)") comp_or_orig <- compare_vcv_models(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), "Log OR (Original Only)") comp_or_comb <- compare_vcv_models(db_or, "Log OR (Combined)") # 3. Bind all comparisons together vcv_comparison_results <- bind_rows( comp_hedges_new, comp_hedges_orig, comp_hedges_comb, comp_or_new, comp_or_orig, comp_or_comb ) ``` ::: ### Sensitivity Comparison Table The interactive table below compares the pool estimate, standard error (SE), 95% Confidence Intervals, and p-values for both models across all subsets. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Key Methodological Findings 1. **Marginal Shifts in Pooled Estimates:** Treating effect sizes as completely independent (Without VCV) causes very minor changes to the overall pooled estimates. For the combined dataset, the Hedges' $g$ estimate shifts from $0.46$ to $0.48$, while the Log Odds Ratio (lnOR) estimate shifts from $0.60$ to $0.64$. This indicates that the main directional conclusions of the meta-analyses are robust to VCV specification. 2. **Standard Error Shifts:** In this multilevel model specification (which partitions variance using nested random intercepts), omitting the VCV matrix and assuming independence leads to a *slight inflation* of the standard error and wider confidence intervals for the combined analyses: * **Hedges' $g$ (Combined):** SE increases from $0.07$ to $0.07$ (CI width increases from $0.29$ to $0.30$). * **Log Odds Ratio (Combined):** SE increases from $0.14$ to $0.15$ (CI width increases from $0.54$ to $0.59$). This demonstrates that accounting for known within-study covariance via `vcalc` yields more precise, correctly-weighted estimates. 3. **Impact on Statistical Significance:** For continuous outcomes (Hedges' $g$ Combined), the pooled estimate is statistically significant with the VCV matrix ($p < 0.0001$) and statistically significant without it ($p < 0.0001$). Binary outcomes (Log OR Combined) are statistically significant under both specifications ($p < 0.0001$ with VCV; $p < 0.0001$ without). Therefore, we conclude that while the overall direction and magnitude of the estimates are stable, **incorporating the VCV matrix** ensures appropriate weighting, controlling for within-study correlation, and obtaining accurate standard errors and statistical tests. --- ## Session Info ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sessionInfo() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` R version 4.6.0 (2026-04-24) Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin23 Running under: macOS Tahoe 26.5.1 Matrix products: default BLAS: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.6/Resources/lib/libRblas.0.dylib LAPACK: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.6/Resources/lib/libRlapack.dylib;
